@thomaspurchas Client updates end up in `AccessoryDriver.set_characteristics`, which is where `char.set_value` is called. Currently, there is no (easy) way to indicate failure of this. I can propose two things:
1. `set_value` returns True or False, which is checked in `set_characteristics`
2. `set_value` throws something , which is catches in `set_characteristics`

In both cases the user should be able to plug into `set_value`. So, again, here are a few options:
- Use the current setter_callback - not perfect because the `self.value` is set before this is called. We can of course change the flow.
- Use some other mechanism

Personally, I think changing the flow in `set_value` as
```
...
success = self.callback(new_value)
if not success:
    return False
# continue as before
```
will be flexible enough, but some input will mean a lot. Thoughts?

To clarify: I'm not suggesting that the kwargs get passed to paramiko, just for this test function, so you'd end up with:

```python
def _create_shell_with_wrong_port(**kwargs):
    return spur.SshShell(
        username=USERNAME,
        password=PASSWORD,
        hostname=HOSTNAME,
        port=54321,
        missing_host_key=spur.ssh.MissingHostKey.accept,
        **kwargs
    )
```

That way, the other usages of `_create_shell_with_wrong_port` continue to pass no value for `sock`, rather than passing a value of `None`, which is closer to common usage. I think having an explicit `sock` argument in the actual constructor for `SshShell` is entirely correct.

Maybe my understanding is flawed but in order to be able to bring back this value on the CPU to self.sum_metric, we will need to do a wait_on_read on the pred_label ?
If we have a loop that has:
```
for batch in data:
   ...
   metric.update()
```
we will wait for the label_pred to be computed before we load the next batch on the GPU.

Whilst if we had `self.sum_metric = nd.zeros(1, ctx=ctx)`
`self.sum_metric += (pred_label.flatten() == label.flatten()).sum()`
then we wouldn't be blocking on this operation before loading the next batch of data and it would just enqueue more operations on the back-end.

I don't think these new functions are necessary.

I use NDArrayDLManager whose member is a copy of NDArray, which increases refcount.
The deleter of DLPack is to delete NDArrayDLManager, which decreases refcount.

If a new NDArray() created by DLPack, the deleter of DLPack will be called by the new NDArray. Similarly, If a PyTorch Tensor createed by DLPack (MXNet), the deleter will be called by the new PyTorch Tensor.

NDArray (CPP and Python class) doesn't have deleter API, so I set the DLPack as the member of NDArray (Python). When NDArray generated by DLPack releases, the DLPack object in the NDArray will release, then the deleter function in DLPack will be called.
Also, these are kind of big functions. Either the compiler won't really inline them, or it's going to generate a huge amount of extra code.

diff --git a/tests/python/pants_test/util/test_meta.py b/tests/python/pants_test/util/test_meta.py

+++ b/tests/python/pants_test/util/test_meta.py
@@ -53,6 +53,11 @@ class OverridingValueField(WithProp):
   _value = 4
 
 
+class OverridingValueInit(WithProp):
+  def __init__(self, v):
+    self._value = v
+
+
 class OverridingMethodDefSuper(WithProp):
 
   _other_value = 2
@@ -77,6 +82,9 @@ class ClassPropertyTest(TestBase):

+  def test_override_inst_value(self):

+

     self.assertEqual(5, OverridingMethodDefSuper.some_property)

Thank you! Twisted has a different approach to Exception handling. I think that must have been what drove my initial approach of logging errors rather than raising them. On reflection, as your comments show, that's not the right approach. I'm going to work through this next week to come up with something that handles errors better. 

In the Subscription class in events_twisted, the twisted method that sends HTTP requests (used by the subscribe, unsubscribe and renew methods) returns a [Deferred](https://twistedmatrix.com/documents/current/core/howto/defer-intro.html). If an Exception occurs inside a function that returns a Deferred, a [Failure](http://twistedmatrix.com/documents/current/api/twisted.python.failure.Failure.html) object is passed to the first errback that has been registered for that deferred with [Deferred.addErrback](https://twistedmatrix.com/documents/current/api/twisted.internet.defer.Deferred.addErrback.html).

I think the subscribe, unsubscribe and renew methods need to return a Deferred in the events_twisted version and to return None in the events version. Then, in the twisted version, the calling code can use addErrback to deal with failures.

Additionally, there should probably be a 'lenient' flag that, if set, will cause the subscribe, unsubscribe and renew methods to log Exceptions rather than raise them. The lenient flag will be used by internal calls. Autorenew is the example we have been discussing, but there's also automatic unsubscribe at exit.

I haven't yet thought through a solution to service.subscribe. This returns a Subscription object, after first calling its subscribe method. In the non-twisted version, if an Exception occurred during the HTTP request call in Subscription.subscribe, it could be caught and handled by the upstream code that called service.subscribe. 

In the twisted version, there would instead be an unhandled failure. The calling code receives a Subscription object, not a Deferred. Therefore, the calling code cannot register an errback to receive and handle the failure. One possibility would be to set a property of the Subscription object to refer to such a failure, so that the calling code could check that property after calling service.subscribe().

I'm trying to follow "python requests" here.

https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/87704105af65b382b86f168f6a54192eab91faf2/requests/auth.py#L71

Everything derives from requests.AuthBase, and is called SomethingAuth.  I'd call one of these requests.Auth instances an Authentication Manager.  Its job in life is to make sure that whenever someone makes a request using requests.Session.post() or similar, it is authenticated using whatever auth technique the Authentication Manager implements.

So for instance, the HTTPBasicAuth class implements the "Basic Auth" in RFC7617.  The iControlRESTTokenAuth implements the token authentication scheme described in the linked devcentral article.

I think there's some value to keeping the ...Auth naming scheme because this is a SomethingAuth class just like everything in requests.auth.  But what comes before, I'm open to suggestions.  I was similarly following iControlRESTSession in session.py.  (I also think Token should be in there somewhere because this is not the most-commonly-documented authentication scheme for BIG-IP).

But really, I'm very much open to suggestions for other names.

I thought the hash of original the source code was appended at the end of the bytecode, but it's actually the [hash of the metadata](https://solidity.readthedocs.io/en/v0.4.24/metadata.html?highlight=hash#encoding-of-the-metadata-hash-in-the-bytecode).

Forget about the swarm hash, another alternative is to add the hash of the bytecode ourselves in the settings. E.g.:

```sh
$ solc raiden=contracts --combined-json bin contracts/TokenNetwork.sol 2>/dev/null | jq '.["contracts"]["contracts/TokenNetwork.sol:TokenNetwork"]["bin"]' | python -c 'import sys,raiden.utils,binascii; print(binascii.hexlify(raiden.utils.sha3(binascii.unhexlify(sys.stdin.read()[1:-2]))))'
b'1a1c260c1c1c9c61b3a5b916af3da7360b42f467fd3827db248ae1af7b8dad30'
```

> But assuming we find a good value for MAXIMUM_PENDING_TRANSFERS then the calculation would be correct.

No, that is not how it works, `GAS_USED_UNLOCK_1_LOCKS * MAXIMUM_PENDING_TRANSFERS` is always wrong.

`GAS_USED_UNLOCK_1_LOCKS` is the gas cost for **one** transaction that unlocks one lock, if you multiply it by `MAXIMUM_PENDING_TRANSFERS` you will have the gas cost of `n` **transactions**, which is completely different from *one* transaction which unlocks `n` locks.

The most obvious difference is that sending `n` transaction will have to pay the minimum `21000` gas cost `n` times.
